U.S. President Donald Trump and Iran are trading threats, oil prices are surging, and a U.S. strike on an Iranian bridge is raising fears the conflict could drag on. Cara Angeline Oliver reports.","duration":135.33,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"Dozens of countries sought ways to restart vital energy shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, as U.S. President Donald Trump and Iran created  escalating threats, driving oil prices sharply higher and deepening anxiety across global markets. In a social media post on Thursday, Trump warned Tehran to make a deal before it is too late and shared what he claimed to be a video of a U.S.  strike on a newly constructed bridge linking Tehran to the northwestern suburb of Karaj.  This eyewitness footage shows smoke rising from the B1 Highway Bridge. Iranian state media said the unfinished bridge was hit on Thursday, killing eight people and wounding dozens. Iran's Foreign Minister condemned the attack, saying the strikes on civilian infrastructure would not force Iran to surrender. We are going to hit them extremely hard over the next two to three weeks. In a speech the night before, Trump repeated threats against Iran's civilian power plants and said U.S. forces would intensify  operations over the coming weeks, drawing vows of retaliation from Tehran. Nearly five weeks into the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, the conflict is spreading instability across the region. Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz has disrupted a fifth of the world's global oil and liquefied natural gas trade. Britain's Foreign Minister Yvette Cooper chaired talks with around 40 countries on restoring freedom of navigation.   We have seen Iran hijack an international shipping route to hold the global economy hostage. But diplomats say no agreement has been reached. Iran, meanwhile, warns this trade could remain closed long-term to the United States and Israel. The standoff has sent oil prices soaring. U.S. crude jumped more than 11 percent Thursday, while Brent surged nearly 8 percent, as traders priced in prolonged supply disruptions. One analyst warned prices could climb above $150 a barrel if shipping is not restored soon. Attention is now turning to the United Nations, where the Security Council is set to vote on a Bahrain- backed resolution to protect commercial shipping in the Strait. Yet, veto-wielding China has made it clear its opposition to authorizing any use of force."},"published_time":"2026-04-03T08:33:51Z","updated_time":"2026-04-03T08:33:51Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69cf7ae2e4b0b60af064a311","caption":"Tensions are escalating in the Middle East as the world looks for ways to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Ryan Brooks reports.","duration":125.2,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"Iranian forces on Saturday were hunting for a missing U.S. pilot whose warplane went down over Iran the day before. These images were released Friday by Iranian state media, and are said to show fragments of the jet. Officials say it was an F-15E fighter plane like this that was downed, and that two crew members were ejected but one remained missing. Both Washington and Tehran said the plane was hit by fire from a new Iranian air defense system. A state TV broadcast featuring a spokesperson for Iran's Revolutionary Guards said the new system also shot down three U.S. drones and two cruise missiles. Two U.S. officials told Reuters that two Black Hawk helicopters looking for the downed plane were hit by Iranian fire but made it out of Iranian airspace. The scale of that crew’s injuries was unclear, but it highlights the risk that remains for U.S. and Israeli craft over Iran,  Despite remarks by U.S. President Donald Trump and his Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth that U.S. forces had total control of the skies. The prospect of a U.S. service member on the run raises the stakes for Washington as the war enters its sixth week. We're going to bring them back to the Stone Ages where they belong. Trump has sent mixed messages since the conflict began, moving between open hostility and hinting at diplomatic progress aimed at ending the war,  Which has killed thousands, set off an energy crisis, and threatened lingering damage to the world economy. A recent Reuters/Ipsos poll showed low public support for the war. On Saturday Trump repeated a threat to intensify attacks on Iran if it failed to reach a deal or open the key Strait of Hormuz.  Writing in a social media post he said,  A post from Iran's foreign minister left the door open for peace talks,  But gave little sign of giving in to U.S. demands, saying Tehran cared about 'terms of a conclusive and lasting end to the illegal war that is imposed on us.'"},"published_time":"2026-04-04T20:22:57Z","updated_time":"2026-04-04T20:44:52Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69d17269e4b01a8b78ae80ee-1775335318065","caption":"Iranian and U.S. forces were searching for a missing American pilot on Saturday from a warplane that was downed over Iran, while President Donald Trump warned Tehran time was running out on his latest deadline for a deal to end the war. This report produced by Jillian Kitchener.","duration":74.46,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"These images, said to show fragments of a US fighter jet shot down over Iran, were released by Iranian State Media on Friday. A U.S. official told Reuters that search-and-rescue efforts have so far rescued one of the two crew members who ejected from the F-15 fighter jet. It is the first known incident of its kind in the nearly five-week-old war.  The Pentagon and U.S. Central Command did not respond to requests for comment. The prospect of U.S. pilots being alive and on the run inside Iran raises the stakes for the United States. An Iranian state news presenter announced a reward to citizens who capture and hand over enemy pilots alive to Iranian authorities. The presenter did not elaborate on the reward. Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps said it was combing an area near where the plane came down in Southwestern Iran. The incident follows threats by U.S. President Donald Trump to bomb the country back to the 'Stone Age,' including to attack Iran's energy infrastructure and desalination plants as he presses Tehran to end the war on U.S. terms."},"published_time":"2026-04-03T19:48:34Z","updated_time":"2026-04-03T19:48:35Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69d01903e4b01a8b78ae8063","caption":"A U.S. fighter jet has been shot down over Iran and search-and-rescue efforts have so far recovered one of two crew members who ejected, a U.S. official told Reuters, in the first known incident of its kind in the nearly five-week-old war. Gas has reached $1.64 per liter. What is a poor man supposed to do? Someone earning $70 to $90 a month relies on his bike for work. What will he do now? All his money goes into gas. He has three children, and there's hunger at home. His family is going without food. Whatever good the government had done was undone after the outbreak of the Iran War. Under pressure, they raised petrol prices, and the poor man has been crushed."},"published_time":"2026-04-03T07:18:45Z","updated_time":"2026-04-03T07:18:47Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69cf695be4b0b60af064a30d","caption":"Pakistanis felt the pinch as the government on Thursday (April 2) hiked consumer prices for diesel and gasoline sharply, its second increase in less than a month, amid rising global oil prices spurred by the conflict in the Middle East.","type":"image","resizer_url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69cf695be4b0b60af064a30d"},"source":{"hls":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/v3/playlist/759588/master.m3u8"},"channels":[{"id":"Article-Playlist-Asia","name":"Article-Playlist: Asia","regions":["US"]},{"id":"Global_Energy","name":"Energy","regions":["US"]},{"id":"Article-Playlist-Middle-East","name":"Article-Playlist: Middle East","regions":["US"]}],"advert_info":{"advert_params":[{"key":"adfriendly","value":true}],"advert_zone":"/4735792/reuters.com/world/middleeast"},"meta_data":[{"key":"upstream-description","value":"Pakistanis felt the pinch as the government on Thursday (April 2) hiked consumer prices for diesel and gasoline sharply, its second increase in less than a month, amid rising global oil prices spurred by the conflict in the Middle East."},{"key":"copyright","value":"(c) 2026 Thomson Reuters, unless otherwise identified. They spoke to Reuters about life in a rickety shack with a thin metal roof offering little protection from falling rocket debris. Diane To reports.","duration":149.76,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"The Ghanem family are among some 32,000 Palestinians estimated to have been forced out of their homes  in West Bank refugee camps by Israel's military last year. They've been living in a rickety one-room shack with a thin metal roof. And things have worsened since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on February 28, exposing the Israeli- occupied West Bank to falling debris, as Israel's interceptors take out Iranian missiles. Madleen Ghanem says her children are terrified by the sound of the rockets  which fly overhead almost daily. 'We don't have shelters. The space where we stay is the same space we hide in. There are no shelters, and no place to run.' Hundreds of pieces of missile debris have fallen on the West Bank since the start of the war, according to Palestinian rescue services. Four Palestinian women were killed last month when a missile hit the West Bank. Ghanem misses their old three-story house, where she lived with her husband's extended family, despite it being in a refugee encampment. Their shack now doesn't even have a proper kitchen or bathroom. This is the family's second displacement. Ghanem's father-in-law ended up in the Tulkarm refugee camp, after fleeing the 1948 war that accompanied the creation of Israel. He now lives with his daughters in a small rented room. One of them, Areej, recalls the day they were forced out from the family home. She says Israeli soldiers broke in without warning in the middle of the night. \"So much suffering,\" she says. \"We didn't take clothes, nothing at all.\" She says their father, who can't walk, had to be dragged out. In early 2025, during a brief truce in fighting with Hamas in Gaza, Israeli forces began demolishing homes and roadways in three longstanding West Bank refugee camps - Tulkarm, Nur Shams and Jenin. Israel said it was to stop militants from exploiting civilian infrastructure. A Human Rights Watch report called the expulsions war crimes and crimes against humanity. The Israeli military did not respond to a request for comment on the Ghanems' case. Though they now live about an hour's walk apart, the family tries to gather each week. Ghanem dreams of reuniting the family under one roof. Her husband, Ibrahim, is bracing for worse to come. \"We expect everything,\" he says. Ryan Brooks reports.","duration":122.96,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"We are going to finish the job, and we're going to finish it very fast. We're getting very close. US President Donald Trump used a primetime speech to reassure a wary public that the war in Iran is nearly finished. Their navy is gone. Their air force is gone. Their missiles are just about used up or beaten. Everyone is talking about it, and tonight I'm pleased to say that these core strategic objectives are nearing completion. On Wednesday, he said that the U.S. military had nearly completed the goals it had set out to accomplish  in its war with Iran and that the conflict would soon be ending. However, he also said the war could escalate if Iran's leaders did not capitulate to U.S. demands during  negotiations, and that the U.S. would continue to strike targets in the weeks ahead. Very shortly. We are going to hit them extremely hard over the next two to three weeks. Trump and his advisors have given shifting explanations and timelines since the U.S. first struck Iran five weeks ago. The latest Reuters/Ipsos poll found that most Americans oppose the war and are upset at rising gasoline prices due to disruptions in the global oil supply. Oil prices climbed more than $4 in the hours after Trump said the U.S. would continue to attack Iran, including energy and oil targets,  over the next few weeks, and did not commit to a specific timeline to end the war. Trump addressed concerns at the pump, saying gas prices would come back down. Many Americans have been concerned to see the recent rise in gasoline prices here at home. This short-term increase has been entirely the result of the Iranian regime launching deranged terror attacks against commercial oil tankers  and neighboring countries that have nothing to do with the conflict. We've had some very bad allies in NATO. Trump did not directly discuss NATO, despite telling Reuters in an interview earlier in the day that he would also express his disgust with the alliance for  what he considers its lack of support for U.S. objectives in Iran."},"published_time":"2026-04-02T03:58:33Z","updated_time":"2026-04-02T03:58:33Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69cde8bae4b0b60af0649e27","caption":"U.S. President Donald Trump told the nation in a televised speech on Wednesday (April 1) night that the U.S. military had nearly completed the goals it had set out to accomplish in its war with Iran and that the conflict would soon be ending. Many Americans have been concerned to see the recent  rise in gasoline prices here at home. This short-term increase has been entirely the result of the Iranian regime launching deranged terror attacks against commercial oil tankers and neighboring countries that have nothing to do with the conflict. This is yet more proof that Iran can never be trusted with nuclear weapons, and the country has been eviscerated and essentially is really no longer a threat. Olivia Zollino reports.","duration":91.06,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"U.S. President Donald Trump told Reuters on Wednesday that the U.S. will end its war on Iran 'pretty quickly' and could return for 'spot hits' if needed. Trump's comments came hours before he was scheduled to make a prime-time address to the nation. Reuters White House correspondent Steve Holland said Trump also spoke about his frustrations with NATO. He said he would express disgust at NATO for not helping open the Strait of Hormuz.  And he said he is absolutely considering withdrawing from the treaty organization. France's junior army minister Alice Rufo said operations by NATO in the Strait of Hormuz would be a breach of international law. Still, Trump has scaled up threats to pull the U.S. out of the NATO alliance if European states did not help stop Iran threatening the waterway. Trump said separately on social media that Iran had asked for a ceasefire but that he would not consider it until Tehran ceased blocking the Strait. Iran denied making any such request. Thousands of people have been killed across the Middle East since February 28, when the U.S. and Israel struck Iran. The conflict has triggered Iranian attacks on Israel, U.S. bases, and the Gulf states, while opening a new front in Lebanon. It has also unleashed one of the largest supply shortages in global energy market history,  Putting political pressure on Trump to end the conflict quickly, even as analysts say his objectives have not been met."},"published_time":"2026-04-01T21:57:39Z","updated_time":"2026-04-01T21:57:41Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69cd9452e4b0b60af0649d36","caption":"The United States will end its war on Iran fairly soon and could return for \"spot hits\" if needed, President Donald Trump told Reuters on Wednesday (April 1), hours before he was scheduled to make a prime-time address to the nation. I wouldn't support him no more, though. And I never supported anyone else other than Trump, no other Republicans. I believe in might, and I believe in strength. So, I think he's doing a good job. I'm an ex-Marine. And so I have a great respect for the office of the presidency as a commander-in-chief.  But we should not be in that war, because Iran has not attacked us. Personally, I don't think we belong over there. Because they've been fighting ever since before Bible days. And they're going to be fighting after we pull out. That's the way I feel about it. All that, I believe it's all for the good. It's just going to be a time period. We've got to be patient and let it work itself out. I think it's tough because people want to trust their leaders and the people that they vote for, and they're being told that there's an imminent threat in Iran. Trump told Reuters on Wednesday (April 1) that he was \"absolutely\" considering withdrawing the U.S. from the pact, a treaty organization ratified by the U.S. Senate in 1949.","duration":87.63,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"The NATO allies committed to dramatically increase their defense spending to that 5% of GDP, something that no one really thought possible. You said you would clarify your stance on Article V just yesterday. Is that something you can explain today? I stand with it. That's why I'm here. If I didn't stand with it, I wouldn't be here. So you would defend countries,  Well, what did I just say? Yeah, of course. Why would I be here? I've long said that, you know, I wonder whether or not NATO would ever be there for us. This was a great test because we don't need them, but they should have been there. And then in the case of NATO, they don't want to help us defend the Strait, and they're the ones that need it.  And I'll say it publicly, we're very disappointed with NATO, because NATO's done absolutely nothing.  And I've always said 25 years ago, I mean, I was somebody,  That wasn't a politician, but I was always involved in politics, and I understood politics. I said 25 years ago that NATO's a paper tiger, but more. Importantly, that will come to their rescue, but they will never come to ours. But I think a tremendous mistake was when NATO just wasn't there. It's going to make a lot of money for the United States because we spent hundreds of billions of dollars a year on NATO, hundreds of protecting them. And we would have always been there for them. Diane To has more.","duration":140.16,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"It was a moment these Gazan mothers had been waiting more than two years for.  Their babies, born prematurely two years ago and evacuated to Egypt, finally came home.  Bissan was one of 11 babies, now toddlers, who were reunited with family in Gaza on Monday as part of a United Nations-organized mission. \"Today is like a birthday, like a new beginning,\" her mother, Sundus Al-Kurd, said. \"And I will make up for everything my daughter was deprived of, God willing.\" In 2023, 29 preterm babies were evacuated from the neonatal intensive care unit at Gaza's Al Shifa hospital. That's when Israeli forces raided the facility,  accusing Hamas of using it for military purposes. The parents were not allowed to go with their babies to Egypt. Al-Kurd said, for almost a year,  she didn't even know if Bissan was still alive. She spoke of the heartache of not being able to hold her daughter for two and a half long years. The reunion was enabled by a U.S.-brokered deal in October that halted most fighting. Israeli attacks have destroyed and damaged medical facilities and neonatal units across Gaza. Israel says Hamas and other militants were using the hospitals to store weapons and hide fighters.  The groups deny this. What is left is \"a big shortage of medications for premature infants,\" according to Mohamed Abu Selmia, director of Al Shifa Hospital,  as well as ventilators and oxygen.  Abu Selmia said seven of the 29 infants evacuated died while in Egypt, and the remaining 11 who did not return to Gaza were with family. For Al-Kurd, bringing Bissan home filled  a void in her life, after an Israeli airstrike on their home in North Gaza killed another daughter and nine other family members. Bissan was delivered prematurely that day,  with Al-Kurd, then eight months pregnant, wounded in the attack. Speaking from their tent encampment in Gaza City, Al-Kurd spoke of worries - about limited food  toys, and Israel continuing some strikes in Gaza. But, she says, she will try hard to provide for Bissan. \"She still doesn't know who her mother is, who her father is, who her family is,\" Al-Kurd said. \"Hopefully, things will improve with time, and the girl will know us.\""},"published_time":"2026-04-01T15:57:49Z","updated_time":"2026-04-01T15:57:49Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69cd3fe7e4b0b60af0649b96","caption":"A day after premature babies evacuated from a Gaza hospital were finally reunited with their parents under a U.N. mission, one mother spoke to Reuters on Tuesday (March 31) about the heartache of missing her daughter for some two years, the worries of their future, and the joy of the reunion. Sean Hogan has more.","duration":98.4,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"Eight million kilograms of Kenyan tea is stuck in poured warehouses as disruption to shipping routes linked to the Iran War continues, according to the East Africa Tea Traders Association. Tea farmers, like Alex Macharia, are worried about the impact. 'This tea subsector actually supports around millions of people in Kenya, actually small-scale farmers.' 'So if this war continues, we will see so many people going hungry, children dropping out of school and people skipping meals.' Kenya exports an average of 100 million kilograms of tea annually to Middle East markets. Widespread disruption to global shipping through channels like the Strait of Hormuz is starting to hurt. George Omuga is the managing director of the association that runs the Mombasa tea auction. He said losses since March 1st were piling up to the tune of eight million dollars per week. The Middle East takes (buys) an average of 20-25% from this auction and Pakistan which also neighbours Iran buys 40%.  So, we are talking, talking about a market share of 65%.  So, should this war continue, the negative consequences will be more severe to the tea trade and tea value chain players. Tea destined for Pakistan and Egypt was still moving, but only via longer routes. Driving up freight and insurance costs and squeezing exporters' margins."},"published_time":"2026-04-01T12:30:53Z","updated_time":"2026-04-01T12:30:53Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69cd0f5ce4b0b60af0649b2b","caption":"Disruption to shipping routes linked to the Iran war has left about eight million kilograms of tea stuck in warehouses in Kenya's port city of Mombasa for weeks, threatening export earnings and farmer incomes, the head of the East Africa Tea Traders Association said. Rachel Graham has more.","duration":112.63,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"Days before the harvest begins, this South African farmer is at a loss as to where he can source and afford fuel.  'We will probably be using pretty close to 2,000 liters a day. So 2,000 liters a day, 12,000 liters, that's six days. Then you're out of fuel.' Derek Mathews is among thousands of farmers struggling with a double price blow Both fuel and fertilizer costs have shot up as the U.S-Israeli war against Iran continues into a second month. And key energy transit routes like the Strait of Hormuz have been choked, upending global energy markets amid higher crude oil prices. Farmers across South Africa complain that fuel is becoming difficult to source as suppliers grapple with higher demand, logistics constraints, and hoarding of unregulated diesel. 'When I put the order in, the price was still at 18 Rand a liter. Okay? The supplier has not been able to deliver for me.' 'Every second day they come back, and then there's this hold up. They haven't got fuel. The trucks didn't get fuel.' In the weeks since Mathews placed his order, prices have risen by a third to 24 Rand, equivalent to $1.43, per liter. At those prices, some of his crops become less financially viable, he said.  'It doesn't make any financial sense at all to grow maize.' And while fuel industry executives and government officials assured farmers that fuel stocks are sufficient to cover April, with isolated disruptions. A March survey by Agri South Africa found that just under half of farmers who responded couldn't get diesel."},"published_time":"2026-04-01T12:04:11Z","updated_time":"2026-04-01T12:04:12Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69cd0910e4b0b60af0649b01","caption":"South African farmer, Derek Mathews, watches an old petrol pump filling a tractor with diesel on his 1,700 hectare farm, wondering where he can find more fuel supplies and afford rising prices days before harvesting starts. type=\"text/javascript\"><\/script>","audio_url":"https://eleven-public-cdn.elevenlabs.io/audio-native/4755746b1f90e57c3c83176ebb4062380b8a9a1a2e7bf22c8d2d745486357d64/lLFfLjOeQUW79maXvCE0/8Ub7x2UKyv6lalF7Y5xR.mp3"},"excerpt":{"content":"Dozens of countries sought ways to restart vital energy shipments through the Strait of Hormuz on Thursday after <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/us/donald-trump/\">U.S. President Donald Trump</a> vowed more ​aggressive <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/iran/\">attacks on Iran</a>, sending oil prices higher again and deepening strain on consumers.","type":"paragraph"},"content_elements":[{"content":"Dozens of countries sought ways to restart vital energy shipments through the Strait of Hormuz on Thursday after <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/us/donald-trump/\">U.S. President Donald Trump</a> vowed more ​aggressive <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/iran/\">attacks on Iran</a>, sending oil prices higher again and deepening strain on consumers.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"After speculation proved untrue that Trump might discuss ending the war in a speech on Wednesday, the president persisted with ‌threats on Thursday, saying in a social media post: \"IT IS TIME FOR IRAN TO MAKE A DEAL BEFORE IT IS TOO LATE\".","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"He also posted video of the U.S. bombing a newly constructed bridge on Thursday between Tehran and the major northwest suburb of Karaj. The B1 bridge was scheduled to open to traffic this year. According to Iran's state media, eight people were killed and 95 others were wounded in the U.S. attack.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"\"Striking civilian structures, including unfinished bridges, will not compel Iranians to surrender,\" Iran Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi said in a statement.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"In the speech on Wednesday ​night, Trump repeated his threats against Iran's civilian power plants and <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/china/view-investor-reactions-trumps-speech-iran-war-2026-04-02/\">gave no clear timeline</a> for ending hostilities, drawing vows of retaliation from Iran and depressing <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/business/wall-st-futures-slide-trump-signals-tougher-iran-strikes-oil-jumps-6-2026-04-02/\">share prices</a>.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"\"We're going to hit them extremely hard over the next two to ​three weeks,\" Trump said amid mounting domestic pressure to end the conflict. \"We're going to bring them back to the Stone Ages where they belong.\"","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"Nearly five weeks after it started ⁠with a joint U.S.-Israeli aerial assault, the war in Iran continues to spread chaos across the region and roil financial markets, raising the pressure on Trump to find a quick resolution to the conflict.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"Britain chaired a virtual meeting on ​Thursday of some 40 countries to <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/europe/uk-host-talks-with-35-countries-reopening-strait-hormuz-2026-04-02/\">explore ways to restore freedom of navigation</a> that did not produce any specific agreement, although participants agreed that all nations should be able to use the waterway freely, one official said.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"Iran has effectively shut down the ​Strait of Hormuz, which normally carries about a fifth of the world's total oil trade, in retaliation for U.S.-Israeli attacks that began on February 28. The war has caused an increase in oil prices, inflation concerns, supply-chain problems and worries about the impact on the global economy.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"Tehran offered a competing vision for future control of the strait, and said it was drafting a protocol with neighboring Oman that would require ships to obtain permits and licenses.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"\"These requirements will not mean restrictions, but rather to facilitate and ensure safe passage and provide better services to ships that pass through this route,\" ​Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi said, according to the official IRNA news agency.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"An Iranian military spokesperson said the strait would remain closed \"long term\" to the U.S. and Israel.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"The European Union's foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas pushed back against Tehran's plan, saying ​Iran cannot be allowed to charge countries a bounty to let ships pass. \"International law doesn't recognise pay-to-pass schemes,\" wrote Kallas on social media.","type":"paragraph"},{"level":1,"content":"OIL HITS $108","type":"header"},{"content":"Benchmark Brent crude prices <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/china/global-markets-wrapup-1-2026-04-02/\">jumped</a> by about 7% to around $108 per barrel, U.S. bond yields spiked and <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/china/global-markets-wrapup-1-2026-04-02/\">global equity</a> markets gave back ‌gains.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"\"The key question ⁠in all investors' minds is 'When is this going to be over?'\" said Russel Chesler, head of investments and capital markets at VanEck Australia.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"Trump in Wednesday's speech told countries that rely on fuel shipments through the Strait of Hormuz to \"just grab it\".","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"However, European and other states have said they will only help secure the strait if there is a ceasefire.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"\"It can only be done <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/macron-says-it-is-unrealistic-open-hormuz-strait-by-force-2026-04-02/\">in consultation with Iran</a>,\" French President Emmanuel Macron said.","type":"paragraph"},{"id":"CSMDPYCNZVJJBGZNPIBBZHJIUE","type":"graphic","url":"https://www.reuters.com/graphics/GLOBAL-MARKETS/THEMES/jnpwrjelkvw/chart.png","title":"Oil prices surged in March on Iran war supply shock","description":"The chart shows brent crude oil price in USD per barrel","byline":"Reuters Staff","graphic_type":"image","resizer_url":"https://www.reuters.com/resizer/v2/https%3A%2F%2Fwww.reuters.com%2Fgraphics%2FGLOBAL-MARKETS%2FTHEMES%2Fjnpwrjelkvw%2Fchart.png?auth=7f83004f04ba3cea092aba403e3eda78bcc0b873ce6248a42f98c6077d6d1691","preview_image":{"url":"https://www.reuters.com/graphics/GLOBAL-MARKETS/THEMES/jnpwrjelkvw/chart.png"}},{"level":1,"content":"IRAN THREATENS MORE ATTACKS","type":"header"},{"content":"Iran's armed forces responded to Trump with a warning of \"more crushing, broader and more destructive\" attacks in store.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"The war will continue until the \"permanent regret and surrender\" of Iran's enemies, said Ebrahim Zolfaqari, spokesperson for the Iranian military's Khatam al-Anbiya central headquarters, in a statement carried by Iranian media.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"Iran's Fars news agency later ​listed several bridges in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Abu Dhabi and ​Jordan, which host U.S. military bases, as potential targets ⁠for Iran's military in response to the U.S. attack on the B1 bridge. The Revolutionary Guards said they had targeted an <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/middle-east/irans-revolutionary-guards-say-they-targeted-amazon-cloud-computing-centre-2026-04-02/\">Amazon cloud computing centre</a> in Bahrain.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"There are fears the conflict may <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/middle-east/war-meant-break-iran-could-leave-tehran-stronger-gulf-exposed-2026-04-01/\">leave Iran with a stranglehold</a> over Middle East energy supplies now that it has shown that it can block the <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/business/energy/western-powers-were-unable-secure-shipping-red-sea-hormuz-will-be-harder-2026-03-25/\">Strait of Hormuz</a> by targeting oil tankers and attacking Gulf countries hosting U.S. ​troops.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"Gulf states say they reserve the right to self-defence but have refrained from responding militarily to repeated Iranian attacks over the past month, seeking to avoid escalation into ​a far more devastating all-out ⁠Middle East war.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"Iran's parliament was reviewing a bill that would formalise the blocking of vessels from hostile countries passing through the strait and the charging of tolls for others wishing to pass, spokesperson Abbas Goodarzi said.","type":"paragraph"},{"level":1,"content":"STRIKE ON IRAN BRIDGE KILLS 8","type":"header"},{"content":"<a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/middle-east/how-many-people-have-been-killed-us-israel-war-iran-2026-03-31/\">Thousands of people</a> have been killed and tens of thousands injured across the Middle East since the war began, with the head of the International Federation of <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/middle-east/medical-needs-surging-iran-supplies-under-threat-red-cross-warns-2026-04-02/\">Red Cross</a> and Red Crescent Societies delegation saying on Thursday
